content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that will destroy me!!!!!

**Score:** 76
**Body:** Can you guys suggest movies that will absolutely destroy me? Not the typical “tragic” ones where one of the main leads dies after falling in love. I mean movies that completely shatter everything I’ve believed until now , the kind that make me sit with myself for at least 30 minutes just to process what I’ve seen. I want to feel actual regret for not watching them sooner… lol. Do movies like this even exist?

edit: now i have a month's worth of watch list, thank y'all !!! if possible i will come back n share my views 😁😁

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. irreversible

...but you might actually regret it
2. Manchester by the sea
3. About time.

Grave of the fireflies
4. Threads
5. Life is Beautiful - most accurate but also misleading title of all time
6. Lilya 4 Ever
7. A promising young woman.
8. Dancer in the Dark
  - These kind of movie questions show up so often and every time the answer is always Dancer in the Dark. I love bleak, depressing films and I've yet to see anything close to Dancer in terms of impact.

Another post earlier had someone describe it like, "if you want to see a kind, innocent person get absolutely curb stomped by life"...I love that description. ❤️‍🩹
9. Requiem for a Dream, Dear Zachary, Kids

## 2. Uncomfortable films that aren't horror?

**Score:** 76
**Body:** I'm looking for feelings of second-hand embarrassment, maybe stress, just generally uncomfortable and unsettling in a way that isn't necessarily scary.

If you've seen the Scott's Tots episode of The Office then that's the feeling I want.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Happiness
2. Tideland (2005)  
Uncut Gems (2019)
3. [Friendship](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/1239655-friendship) (2025)  
>Suburban dad Craig falls hard for his charismatic new neighbor Austin, as Craig’s attempts to make an adult male friend threaten to ruin both of their lives.
4. Killing of a scared deer and pretty much all Lanthimos movies.

Shiva baby is a comedy movie that's super stressful.
5. Beau Is Afraid. Ari Aster makes his audience feel Beau’s anxiety and discomfort rather than just showing it.
6. Nightcrawler with Jake Gyllenhaal. One of the few movies that I just couldn't finish because of how much I hated the main character which is not to say that the character is badly written, but actually the opposite. It made me very uncomfortable. It was also the movie that made me realize how amazing of an actor Gyllenhaal is.

Edit: come to think of it, it might not fit the description of what you're looking for exactly, but still
7. American Beauty
8. Gummo

Requiem For A Dream
9. Tár. Cate Blanchett’s main character has so many walls that imprison her. it’s very uncomfortable.
10. Leaving Las Vegas

## 3. Best alien movies.

**Score:** 35
**Body:** I’m not talking like Aliens(s) or War of the Worlds.

I’m thinking along the lines of Fire in the Sky, Communion, Arrival.

Less sci-fi/horror aliens 

More abduction/thriller aliens 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Vast of Night

Wish there were more like it
2. Close Encounters Of The Third Kind
3. The Fourth Kind
4. Signs

District 9
5. Under the Skin

No One Will Save You
  - Yes, Under The Skin…good one!
  - Under the Skin is a great answer for this
6. Sputnik comes to mind.
7. Invasion of the Body Snatchers.  The 1950s black and white version and the 1970s remake with Leonard Nimoy and Donald Sutherland are both good.
8. Contact

Super 8

The Vast of Night

## 4. Movies where the main character slowly starts to unravel?

**Score:** 31
**Body:** As title states. The closest I can think of is Fight Club as you slowly watch jack loose his mind or even the Creep series. I'm pretty sensitive to vomit, so I'd like to ask for a heads up if that's applicable. Thanks in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Falling Down
2. The Shining, Black Swan, The Lighthouse, The Machinist, Mulholland Drive, The Deer Hunter, In the Mouth of Madness, Momento, Apocalypse Now, Macbeth, Eraserhead, There Will Be Blood, Take Shelter, Betty Blue, A Beautiful Mind
3. Taxi Driver
4. Beau is Afraid
5. What About Bob?
6. Vampire’s Kiss
7. The Fly.
8. The Aviator and Shutter Island, both with Leo DiCaprio
9. 1408
10. Falling Down

## 5. Lookingfor the best clay animation movie ever

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I’m here to find out what the best clay animation movie of all time is. Thanks in advance for your help! My favorite is Memoir of a Snail.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wallace and Gromit - The Wrong Trousers.
  - The correct answer... The chase scene with the model train is still to this day one of the best things I've ever seen
2. The Wrong Trousers has to sit high on the list.
3. Original Wallace and Gromit trilogy.
4. Idk but fantastic Mr fox is my top stop motion favorite.
5. Wallace and Gromit "A Grand Day Out."
6. Kubo and the Two Strings (2016)

Not 100 pct sure if it is clay, definitely stop motion. Really good movie.

I did like Memoir of a Snail a lot too btw!
7. Mary & Max!!
8. Chicken Run does it for me
9. A Nightmare Before Christmas

Robot Chicken and Celebrity Death Match are good claymation TV shows.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. The girl next door, is it any good?

**Score:** 530

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes, it’s a fun great little movie. 

Stupid but funny with more heart than you would expect
  - Not a bad take at all. This movie is one of my all time favorites partly because of the surprising amount of heart it has as well as because of the pitch perfect soundtrack, the unconventional love story, and of course for my , at the time, teenage mind haha
  - It’s great. Lots of moral fiber.
2. Elisha Cuthbert in the girl next door is my number 1 childhood celebrity crush
3. Yes. The juice is worth the squeeze.
4. Timothy Olyphant makes the film better than it should be.
  - He’s absolutely menacing in this flick
5. Great movie just do not get it mixed up with The Girl Next Door
6. Yes I think it’s a classic. Not much critical thinking required. Just a silly, enjoyable movie
7. Cracking film, as a lower-stakes version of Risky Business goes. Genuinely fun, has genuine heart and great performances all round.

But to me it will always hold the title of \*darkest\* out of nowhere one-liner I've heard compared to how light-hearted the rest of the film is.

(Very mild spoilers)

Later on in the film, two porn stars are introducing a sex-ed film meant for high schoolers and the exchange (roughly) goes;

"Welcome to this sex-education film! Many of you are getting ready for prom! Which means a lot of pressure to lose your virginity! I know I lost my virginity at prom! How about you?!"

"When I was... um... 10"

"Moving on!"

Just a wildly dark joke in the middle of everything else.

## 2. Who Do You Think Is Greatest Antihero In Cinema History?

**Score:** 402
**Body:** My Answer:Captain Jack Sparrow

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mad Max.
2. “The man with no name” character that Clint Eastwood played in several movies
  - Pretty much the definition of antihero
3. Quint in Jaws.
4. Cool Hand Luke
5. While not the greatest, I'll always have a soft spot for Yondu.
  - Marry Poppins Ya'all!
6. Ash Williams.
7. Lebowski
  - You've got the wrong guy... nobody calls him Lebowski,  he's The Dude.

## 3. I Think That One Flew Over the Cuckoo’s Nest Still Holds Up Perfectly Today

**Score:** 395
**Body:** The dialogue is honestly the most timeless and closest to any film today that I have ever seen. The writing for every character is so perfect but never cheesy, Nicholson’s neurotic behavior is toned down juuust enough to craft the perfect performance. Fletcher and Doriff are flawless as well. I think if this film were released today with no changes in the script it would still sweep the Oscar’s and be hailed. I have yet to see a movie from the 70s or earlier that holds up this well. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As a mentally ill person with numerous mental ward visits under the belt...

...I absolutely love this movie.
2. McMurphy would never be able to convince a court today that he belonged in a mental hospital over jail, he’s not nearly as crazy as half the people in jail today are
  - The authorities allowed him an indefinite stay to see if he was really insane. It didn’t take too long for Nurse Ratchet to “evaluate” him. I suspect she quickly found out and chose to keep him there because she was determined to break him, which she unfortunately did.
    - The Psychiatrists think he’s faking. Nurse Ratchet convinces them to let him stay.
3. agree. nurse ratched is the ultimate villain.
  - I would say it’s the system that enables her and gives her that power

The scariest part about Ratched is that people very similar to her exist in the health care profession all over 

People like her are attracted to those kinds of positions. They don’t have power in their own lives so they might as well find a job where they do
4. Top Ten All Time Movie!
5. Being an outsider will always hold up, I think.
6. When I started my psych rotation in nursing school, before we even cracked a book, the instructors had us watch One Flew Over the Cuckoo’s Nest.
7. Ahh , juicy fruit!

## 4. What scene terrified you as a kid?

**Score:** 265
**Body:** 1. The Secret of NIMH, when the spider starts stalking Mrs. Frisby—this scene was the stuff of nightmares.
2. The Land Before Time, when Sarah discovers that the sharptooth is still alive right as she's running into its eye.
3. THX, nothing was ever scarier than that.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The dip scene from Who Framed Roger Rabbit
2. The grand high witches face reveal and her rat transformation in the 1990 adaptation of The Witches
3. The robot woman from Superman 3
4. A whole lot of Watership Down
5. Madam Mim in The Sword in the Stone, when she transforms into a pig
6. I totally forgot about the secret of nimh. Does it still hold up?
  - It absolutely does. Id call it timeless, its always a good watch
7. The child catcher scene in Chitty Chitty Bang Bang.
8. Pee Wee’s Big Adventure. Hitchhiking with the semi truck driver.
9. Gmork the Wolf from Never Ending Story.

Pretty sure they borrowed it from the movie American Werewolf in London..

## 5. What’s a role that changed your opinion on an actor/actress? (Image - Michael B. Jordan, Sinners)

**Score:** 247
**Body:** Now to be clear, I’ve always thought Michael B. Jordan was a solid actor, but he never really blew my mind in a role until I watched Sinners.

He portrayed the twins and their opposite personalities so well that it completely changed my opinion on him as an actor. I think it’s easily his best role so far.

Thoughts?



### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Robert Pattinson in The Lighthouse turned me around. 

Also check him out in GoodTime. Dudes just straight up great.
  - Pattinson's amazing. People keep bringing up Twilight, but let's be honest: everyone's acting sucked in those movies
2. James McAvoy in Split. Not that I ever thought he was a bad actor, but maybe I was just indifferent? After I saw that, I was like wow! That was an incredible performance
3. Steve Carell in Foxcatcher
4. Dave Bautista in Blade Runner 2049. It's a small scene but it shows Bautista's range.
5. I actually think he’s a great actor (and has gotten better as the years went on) but Brad Pitt in Burn After Reading as a comedy actor. I’d never seen him in a comedic role before then and he killed it in that movie. Stand out role for me.
  - George Clooney also hits different in Coen flicks.
6. Robert Downey Jr. in tropic thunder comes to mind.
  - “For 400 years…that word has kept us down.”

“….What the fuck?”
7. Colin Farrell- In Bruges

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. 'One Battle After Another' is even better after a second viewing

**Score:** 198
**Body:** Saw it yesterday and woke up thinking about it, so went and saw it again (in IMAX) today.

I'd love to read the screenplay.  Want to understand every aspect of it that makes it so good.

My first thought is that the pacing has a lot to do with it.  It feels so much shorter than its run time, and PTA is an economical filmmaker, which shows itself in part in a lack of a need to spell everything out.

And then obviously the way in which he seems to have captured a current moment, which is often rare because of the time it takes to make a studio feature film (even though this is loosely based on Pynchon from 1990 and PTA says he started writing this in 1999...)

I dunno.  I just wanna talk with other people who were into it about how good it is. :)

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I have this theory that you haven’t really seen a movie until you’ve seen it twice. The first time you’re mostly just absorbing the story, admiring shots, etc. The second time, you can understand the structure without just being carried along by the story. This was particularly true of two of Scorsese’s most recent films, The Irishman and Silence. The first time, I thought they were great. The second time, I thought they were perfectly constructed masterpieces. Wasn’t as big a fan of Killers of the Flower Moon, though.
  - Completely agreed on your Scorsese 2nd-watch syndrome. That being said, Killers went from pretty good to masterpiece for me.

Also felt the same way about the first Dune, The Master, Inglourious Basterds, Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, No country for old men, and Chinatown. Unfortunately, Boogie Nights has not lived up to second viewing standards as I am just lukewarm on the movie.
2. One detail i thought was really cool was the scene where the young actress who plays Dicaprio's character's daughter fires the entire belt of machine gun ammunition. She does it with her eyes open the whole time. Normally in films when actors fire a weapon, they flinch slightly, she doesn't.

Which means they must have trained the heck out of her for that scene.
  - I'm not sure if it was an intentional call back, but in the early montage, while her mother is pregnant with her, she expends a whole clip from a similar gun directly beside her belly. That sound would probably be pretty distinct in her memory, even if she isn't conscious of it.
    - Her mom evens mentions something about the baby in her womb being able to "hear everything" or something like that. I can't exactly remember what she said but it gave the impression that those experiences were shaping the child in some way.
3. Saw it for the second time today as well. I’m an Editor by trade so I can’t help but to look at the movie a little differently and I really love that this movie starts with two montage sequences, although calling them a montage may be confusing for some or even wrong. Hear me out. They’re not montages like a training montage in a Rocky movie or a kung-fu flick — they’re montages in the sense that they’re individual scenes that are joined together by a common music cue. It’s something that Christopher Nolan movies do very well when he’s intercutting stories and timelines and everything else and it’s a technique I’ve used as a secret weapon cutting unscripted shows. Once you notice it, you can’t unsee the way music links the scenes together thematically and it FLOWS as a sequence. I call it “letting the music do the work”, and I like that this movie starts with the entire Perfidia Beverly Hills sequence and doesn’t give you a moment to breathe until it’s 16 years later. Next time you watch it, notice which scenes play without music. Just so well done. Both times I’ve seen it with a vocal crowd that goes DEAD SILENT during the third act, you can FEEL everyone completely locked in. The characters stop talking and the entire audience, different backgrounds, different walks of life, different senses of humor — completely transfixed. One of the coolest experiences in a movie theater this year.
  - > Next time you watch it, notice which scenes play without music.

I've only seen it once but the scene where Lockjaw is handing Willa over to the hired killer/driver guy really stood out for this reason. It was an especially tense scene in an already really tense movie, underscored by the fact that the soundtrack goes completely silent. The absence of a score was so "loud" during that scene.
4. One extremely minor thing that bothered me about this movie is that nobody ever closes the car door before they take off. It happens several times throughout the movie and felt like a really strange choice.
  - I like when Willa does this and the camera attached to the door swings towards her as she takes off.
  - I loved that detail lol. You can do the same thing in the GTA games when you steal a car and slam on the gas.

## 2. It’s impressive how much of The Godfather Part II’s story is not told through dialogue, but Al Pacino’s eyes

**Score:** 90
**Body:** The Godfather Part II is a masterclass of ‘show, don’t tell’ and Al Pacino delivers a tour de force performance. What I love is how different the movie feels compared to the action-packed, plot-driven pace of Part I. This is broader, more complex, more subtle. It’s hard to know if this film falls apart if you don’t have Al Pacino because so much is told with just his eyes and facial expressions. 

- Michael realises Fredo sold him out. A subtle moment, all it takes is Fredo referring to a shared moment between him and a man he pretended not to know (Johnny Ola) and you see Michael’s look of discovery, disbelief and then grief all minutes before the famous ‘I know it was you, you broke my heart’ moment. 

- Michael’s eyes basically scream murderous rage at Kay while she reveals the abortion of their son

- Michael glances at Al Neri while hugging Fredo, revealing his forgiveness is purely a show. Just this glance is as obvious an order to kill Fredo as if he directly told Al Neri.

- The end of the film where nothing is said, we just see Michael sit in a chair as he stares off. We see and understand the thematic climax of the film and Michael’s entire arc just with that million mile away stare into the distance. What more subtle way to show he realises he’s lost his soul. 

I’ve not seen a better performance (or a better movie for that matter.) But interested to hear more thoughts and discussion

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 70s Al Pacino was just incredible. The actor he became from the 80s onwards, while often very compelling, is like a completely different person. 

And Michael's an incredible character. Outwardly calm, but always with a torrent of repressed rage just under the surface. He's a calculating businessman, rather than a charmer like his father. The eyes really sell that repression.
  - Pacino seems to enjoy playing shouty and aggressive characters like in Scent of a Woman or the Devil's Advocate. Personally, I don't like that style as much. He's very good at playing thoughtful and subtle parts, and that's a more rare skill.
  - I saw Pacino on Broadway. In 2016, he starred in a run of a play written by David Mamet: *China Doll.* I was psyched. Mamet? Pacino? This could be amazing!

It was the worst play I have ever seen in my life, at least in terms of professional productions. Pacino's performance was a mess.

A good 75% of the play was Pacino's character talking to people over the phone. Except it wasn't an actual phone, but a Bluetooth earpiece. And you never hear another voice the entire time. It was a whole lot of him repeating something the person on the other end of the call said, then reacting to it. Something that can be workable in small increments -- a quick call that gets summarized to other characters by the person that answers it. But he was *mostly* the only person on the stage (there was a single other character in the play).

No drama, no tension, no real investment in anything that was going on. 

I don't know who decided that a mechanism for good drama would be having an overwhelming majority of the play be a one-sided conversation, but it was a dumpster fire and a miserable failure. If that kind of thing *could* be pulled off, Pacino is not the actor to do it (or at least he isn't anymore).

A waste of time, talent, and good money. At least my wife and I had a fun night out in the city. So it wasn't a total loss.
  - Pacino has been stuck in "Scarface mode" ever since making that film - he's spent the past 40-odd years at full intensity whenever possible
    - Watch him as Ricky Roma in Glengarry Glen Ross if you haven’t already. Yes he has his shouty scene but a lot of his performance is beautifully subtle as he plays a salesperson essentially seducing a guy into taking a bad deal. He’s incredible in that film.
2. It's pretty genius to have most of Michael's decisions being made offscreen in this one. I can imagine audiences might still be waiting for the moment Michael *really* breaks bad, something even worse than the baptism murders.

But he is, in fact, already broken. Making his ultimate betrayal a foregone conclusion within the structure of the film forces the audience to search his face for minutes at a time, trying to figure out how the Corleones are going to come out on top of this corporate 'legitimization' but truly not wanting Michael to feed his descent any further.
  - Yes the tragedy comes from the characters acting according to their natures, and according the rules of the society they live in. Maybe things could have played out better if Vito made Tom Hagen the Don, but as an adopted son, that was never going to happen.
    - I think most importantly it comes from the machismo code of the family, the thing the sons all hold dearest. Vito is the impossible leader to try to live up to, and they all fail in the most key aspect of his code which is to put family above all else. Kay getting the abortion is the ultimate proof of him abandoning what he was, someone that saw the underlying evil of their lifestyle and rebelled by joining the military, something he thought was noble but Sonny saw as a family betrayal. Ironically it’s Fredo that is the only person to congratulate him on his decision. You’re right that Hagen seems to be the only one that truly lives up to what Vito wanted Michael to be, which is why Michael pushes him out. He’s already ashamed.
3. The “banana daiquiri” scene with Fredo is just as vital and plays into the scene where Michael realizes it was Fredo who betrayed him. If you watch his eyes, Michael is watching and testing Fredo just like he did with everyone else post-assassination attempt.

It’s not hard to imagine that part of Michael’s shocked reaction was because he thought he had read Fredo and decided to trust him. And subsequently he grows colder to everyone around him because he refuses to put himself in a position to trust someone like that ever again.
4. I may be in the minority but I vastly prefer Pacino’s storyline in part 2 over DeNiro’s. Wish 3 was never made, as his arc in the first two films is absolute peak. 

Insane Pacino didn’t win any Oscar’s for those

## 3. What are some beautiful films that don't feel bleak and dark and depressing? What's the polar opposite, yet beautiful? I really need to get out of this negative headspace.

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Think about your favourite films, it's pretty hard to stay away from the dark stuff, right? Or is it just me?  
  
I'm trying to divert from my usual patterns and maybe I'm just looking for something that's actually very rare. I like **beautiful** films that really **captivate** me - and unfortunately that's usually accomplished by them draining my soul (or at least nibbling on it a bit). I just like crushing and bleak stuff because it makes me feel something and that kinda sounds like self-harm now that I type it out.  
  
When I stumble upon positive and life-affirming stuff it's usually at least tinted in melancholy; and if it's just cute and happy, it's usually not as beautiful and artistic as I'd like it to be. It seems like film just thinks it has to be more earnest and heavy to be taken serious as art.

Some of my favourites are: *Dead Man, Apocalypse Now, Biutiful, Lost In Translation, Léon: The Professional, Buffalo 66, Koyaanisqatsi, Lux Aeterna, Julien Donkey Boy, Grave of The Fireflies, Mandy, Antichrist, Mulholland Drive, A Woman Under The Influence, M, Last and First Men, Beau Travail...* Even the ones that don't put all emphasis on being crushing have very depressing backdrops or just take misery and death for granted and weave the plot around that.

**Help me get away from Lars and Gaspar and all those other evil men.**

Some more light hearted beautiful ones I could think of right now: Porco Rosso, The Fall, Samsara (2023), The Electrical Life of Louis Wain, Il Buco (which nobody has seen I think)... So yeah, also those aren't really feel good at all. I guess it's that contrast that fascinates me so often? Maybe it's cute animals and nature that could do the trick? Quirky stuff à la Wes Andersen and Taika Waititi tends to annoy me by the way. I like baby pigs and maybe would like to see life from their point of view.

Tank you all!



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Singin' in the Rain has been my go-to since I was in my early 20s. It's a joy, but it's also important as a film. It make me feel good, but also allows me to marvel from an artistic standpoint, which also always puts me in a more hopeful state of mind.

When I was also younger, Amelie was one of those films that fit this bill. It overflows with charm and silliness, which is the stuff of youth, and youth is potential. It is now.

Harvey. It's a serious subject wrapped in innocence and perspective and compassion. And it's genuinely funny. The lightheartedness combined with Jimmy Stewart giving the performance of a lifetime always leaves a warm impression every time.
2. Harold and Maude veers from dark comedy to true darkness to hilarious campiness to incredible vulnerability. It's the ultimate life-affirming movie. 

And I haven't seen this one personally, but maybe Fanny and Alexander would fit the bill??

ETA--oh, and Ponyo! 
3. Juzo Itami's "Tampopo" is one of the smartest, funniest, most profound, and joyful films I've ever seen. It has very little melancholy, it is one of the great joyous experiences in cinema that has as much to say about the human condition as any film ever has.

It also has, IMO, the greatest final shot in the history of cinema.
  - Came here to say this. One of the best films of all time. Felt like a celebration of food and life, with acceptance of the sad parts as well.
4. I hear you.  Here are some movies to think about:

Oh Brother Where Art Thou - A silly and meandering retelling of the Odyssey set in the American south.  So many funny lines and ridiculous situations.

Kung Fu Hustle - An outrageous take on the traditional Kung Fu movie.   It keeps you guessing as a band of oppressed underdogs and losers fight against gangsters and for true love.  You haven't seen anything else like this movie.

Flow - A silent animated movie starring a cat navigating a fantastical land in the wake of a world altering flood.  It's beautiful and moving and utterly captivating.

Straight Story - David Lynch's understated movie about family love, redemption, and perseverance.  It's quiet, sincere, and uplifting.

Playtime - This a clockwork of a movie, with the main character bumbling through modern Paris from one sight gag to another.  It's nearly silent and funny throughout.

Babe - You're the one who mentioned pigs, so I'm just throwing this out there.
  - The Straight Story might be my favorite Lynch movie, and I'm aware of how lame that is. But there's something so satisfying about seeing him tee up a totally normal movie, absolutely *crush* it out of the park, not a hint of mawkishness or falsity, and then go back to doing regular David Lynch stuff. 

It's like seeing stuff from Picasso's early career. There's that realization of "oh, he's not just making weird box people because that's all he can do -- he can do *everything*, he just *wants* to make weird box people."
5. It's a tough question when you start to think about it. I thought of some of the more recent "prestige" action movies, like Fury Road or Dune, but I don't really think that's what OP is looking for. Here's a few:

O Brother Where Art Thou? - looks like nothing else and always leaves me feeling good

True Stories - kind of awkward and tedious at times but I think it's beautiful

To Catch a Thief - it may not be all that life-affirming or inspirational, but it's at least breezy and fun, and it looks gorgeous

Lord of the Rings - although there's a lot of violence and death, the story itself is pretty uplifting and there's some iconic imagery in there.

And there's always old classic stuff like The Wizard of Oz or The Adventures of Robin Hood, if that's more to your taste.
6. I recently dove into some 1930s films because I realized how foreign they seem to me, culturally. I was delighted by how hilarious and entertaining and frankly modern they felt. Blown away by the inventive cinematography. They're also fascinating historical capsules for humor and social norms etc, as someone who doesn't need or look for cutesy feel good films. A couple great ones:


It Happened One Night (basically the first rom-com, really funny with great acting)


The Old Dark House


Animal Crackers


A Night At the Opera 
7. >When I stumble upon positive and life-affirming stuff it's usually at least tinted in melancholy; and if it's just cute and happy, it's usually not as beautiful and artistic as I'd like it to be. It seems like film just thinks it has to be more earnest and heavy to be taken serious as art.

I get what you say.

Did you watch One Battle After Another? Now, I know everyone's talking about it. But the reasons I give:

- it's a movie, PTA knows it, the movie knows it, it's a spectacle, it's fun, engaging and sentimental, and overall a good theater experience and I'm telling you this because going put to the theater is essential—at least for me, changes my whole mood, it's a good place to get out of your head.

- it isn't bleak, and PTA has made some bleak films in the past. It doesn't ignore the terrible, but also doesn't linger on the bleak like many movies do nowadays.
8. I think what you're looking for is quite rare and more easily would be found in either books or European movies as classic Hollywood with stunning operator work usually would mean a challenging story. 


If you're OK with subtitles and a little bit of research, I strongly recommend Mon Oncle by Jacques Tati and The Red Balloon by Albert Lamorisse which is one of the most beautiful films ever.

What' you could do is to continue just researching into great films but read reviews more carefully and try to see if the film is going to crash your soul. I think reading two Buffalo 66 reviews would give you a good hint, and some of the others as well.


I also don't like the negativity so I focus on comfort and comedy films but it seems you like dramas more. What you're looking for is probably period dramas as they tend to be beautifully shot and many of them tend to have a more positive overall vibe.

## 4. I've been wanting to write this review for about a year, but I never had the energy for it. " Our parents are not just our parents ".. Aftersun

**Score:** 35
**Body:** The Film's Story:

The film tells the story of Sophie, a woman reflecting on a holiday she spent in Turkey with her young father, Calum, twenty years ago. At the time, she was unaware of his struggle with depression. The journey reveals his inner sadness and conflicts, which she only came to understand as an adult. The film explores Sophie's memories, a blend of truth and imagination, as she tries to understand the father who was an enigma to her and reconcile with his past to better know herself.

**My First Viewing:**

I first saw this film before I left my family and moved away due to circumstances in my life, around the end of 2022, just after it premiered at festivals and on streaming platforms. Back then, I didn't find it particularly special. I watched it online, gave it three stars, and that was that.

**My Second Viewing & Personal Reflection:**

Later, when I started living alone, I began to rethink my relationship with my parents. I started seeing them as human beings separate from me—not just as "Mom and Dad," but as people who were living life for the first time, just like me. They had their own stories, lives, and experiences that shaped them long before I existed. This perspective made me overlook some of the problems from my childhood. I even realized that when we texted on the phone, we expressed our feelings more freely because of the distance, unlike when I lived with them.

This reminded me of a saying: "Our parents haven't lived seven thousand years; this is their first time living life, just like us." It's possible they faced situations they didn't know how to handle and made mistakes with us. All of this thinking brought me back to *Aftersun*, and I found myself deeply relating to it this time.

My second viewing was in a cinema. Even though I had already seen it, I felt a need to watch it surrounded by people. The theater was packed because the film had gained fame for impressing the critics.

**Understanding the Film's Core:**

And then I truly grasped the film's story: Sophie, whose father committed suicide after their last vacation together. She was angry at him for it. As a child, she didn't understand the reasons; to her, he was just normal. She thought his yoga was just "weird moves." There was a distance between them, visually represented by the camera in some scenes. She couldn't remember everything perfectly, so she had to fill those gaps in her memory, aided by the camcorder footage from their holiday.

**What I Loved About the Film:**

What I appreciated most was that the film never directly stated the reason for Calum's suicide. Instead, we discover it alongside Sophie. We see scenes of him crying, hints of his financial struggles, his separation from his wife, and his own suffering with his father during his childhood. This background made him strive to provide everything he could for Sophie, despite his poverty, so she wouldn't feel the lack he felt as a child—something Sophie herself notices and mentions to him in one poignant scene. The scene where he breaks down after Sophie celebrates his birthday, which clearly triggered childhood trauma, was especially powerful.

The film speaks to the gap between children and parents. A child sees a parent as someone who is supposed to provide for them, often failing to see them as a human being with a full life, burdens, and a personality that was formed long before they became a parent. Sophie tries to see Calum as the person she didn't understand when she was young, the person she was angry at for leaving her. She re-evaluates him through the lens of her own adulthood and experience as a mother.


The film ended, and I spontaneously started clapping. Soon, the entire theater joined me.
10/10.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of the reasons this film does well with repeated viewings is there is a ton of subtext to analyze. For example a likely part of why Sophie is revisiting the footage and her memories now is she is the mother of a newborn. She has gained a different perspective but what do we read from that? Is she feeling anger at her dad for abandoning her, feeling she could never do that to her own child? Is she feeling the same depression that took hold of Calum (her own possible issues with depression shown earlier in the film)? Or is she re-evaluating her place in the world as a mother and a daughter now that she’s roughly the same age as her dad when he killed himself? Or is it all of that? It leaves it up to the viewer to fill in those gaps which can be frustrating in a film that’s not this masterful. It puts the viewer in the place of Sophie in that memories are piecemeal and by their nature subject to personal interpretation (which can change over time).

Another example is that my interpretation of the birthday scene is different - I think that Calum deeply hates himself and finds others celebrating him jarring. You then realize with horror that when he moves past the open self-hatred of most of the film and can let go (e.g., the last dance) it’s because he’s finally set on committing suicide (a real-life phenomena of people becoming happier when they’ve made their decision to act).
  - One thing that I don’t see mentioned enough is: it’s her birthday. Shes watching all this on her bday and thinking about it on her bday. But why? Its never explicitly stated but my theory is that she has just turned the age her father was when he died. Or maybe one year older… she has officially lived as long or longer than he did. I know from friends and loved ones who have lost their parents at a young age that the birthday when you turn the same age as your parents were when they died is tough.
2. I have a slightly different take- who Callum is and why he's about what he is, is irrelevant compared to his role in Sophie's life. She may realize later on in life that he had his own thoughts and emotions she never got to know, yes, but it doesn't explicitly state that she forgives him finally and 'gets' him. It's about memory and the way people live in our thoughts and how the way they're represented lives on in good or damaging ways, not unlike the manifestations in Inception or Solaris of how the main character 'sees' the dead loved one who now only exists in their perception as opposed to objective reality. 

Still a great film though, because it makes you think and does not explicitly spell all this out. I don't think you're wrong but I'm not sure it's advocating for that exact message of forgiving dead loved ones, that's an interpretation one can bring to it. I don't not forgive some people I loved for leaving me, but more important is how my relationship to them has continued to evolve even after they're gone, and it's based on the way I last saw them when I was 10. And you can rethink them later on, but that core memory will still be fixed.

## 5. Did anyone else find OBAA underwhelming?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Perhaps I fell for the insane amount of hype and expectations pre-public critics were setting. Many were saying this was a transcendent spectacle, the film of the decade. I came out sort of disappointed. There was a lot to like but a lot of it just didn't feel very strong to me. 

DiCaprio and Del Toro were amazing. The paranoia of Bob continuously being tempered by Sergio was such an interesting dynamic. Honestly, if the film focused more on that dynamic it would have been amazing. I was getting Rick Dalton x Cliff Botth vibes from them. Perhaps I'm not a fan of Pynchon's hyper surrealism, but I just found a lot of the silly elements out of place when we get cuts between Illuminati racist cultists in an old lady's basement, and the gritty pursuit and chase sequences of Bob looking for his daughter. 

Lockjaw's character was just too slapstick for me especially with his dominatrix kink and the over-the-top subplot of him trying to kill his half black daughter becuase he wants to join the racist illuminati. I get the movie is a black comedy, but I just felt there was a more raw and emotional film competing with those moments. 

I still need to work through my feelings on this film. I am a PTA fan and did enjoy the previous entry, Licorice Pizza, which does have some overlap with this recent one, but something just doesn't sit right with me for OBAA. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw There Will Be Blood in theaters back in 2007. It ended and I looked at my friend and said “I’ll never watch that movie again. I hated it so much.”

Then I thought about it every day for like 9 months until the Blu-ray came out. Preordered it. As soon as the Blu-ray arrived, I watched it and then watched it again. TWBB is now a top 5 movie for me.

I’m not saying the same thing will happen for you with this. Just maybe give it some time.

I liked it when I got out of the theater but didn’t think it was the movie of the decade. I’d still put Zone of Interest and Memoria ahead of it. But. It’s definitely grown on me already. 

Maybe you just like stories that are more grounded. I get that. But the contrast between the cult and the gritty pursuit isn’t just superficial. It’s part of the commentary. The tonal gap highlights an actual gap in experience between the people making these decisions and everyone else.

Like think about Trump and all his fuck face advisors sitting in the White House deciding to send ICE somewhere. Then think of the reality of the people targeted by ICE. As hypersurreal as it is in the film, it’s almost more absurd in real life.

[In case you want a deeper dive into the themes](https://filmcolossus.com/one-battle-after-another-explained/)
2. As someone who enjoyed the film, but didn’t think it was anywhere near the best movie of the decade, I do think that early critic reviews claiming extremely bold statements like this before the general public has a chance to see it is setting it up to fall flat with a large portion of its audience. 

Now, I can certainly see how those grand proclamations can help with certain viewers. But I think if you walk out of the theater and you don’t believe it to be the best movie of the decade, you might feel disappointed.

Fun movie for me and I do look forward to seeing what repeat viewings yield.
3. Yeah, even though the film is engaging for a lot of its runtime and has some great characters and sequences, I think it fundamentally didn't really work for me because:

1. Bob is played kinda one-note by DiCaprio, and isn't given an awful lot to do. He's either bumbling around or acting paranoid, often both in the same scene, and we never really get to understand why he joined the French 75 in the first place, whether he truly cared for the cause, and even whether he loved Perfidia and how much. Obviously PTA is very happy to not fill in huge amounts of exposition and let the audience figure things out, but when it's your central character, I felt like so much was missing. And like you said elsewhere, making him an explosives expert and never once seeing him use them after the 15 min mark is like never shooting Chekov's gun.

2. Lockjaw was by far the worst part of the film for me. He was too cartoonish to register as a relevant satire of American military machismo, same with the Christmas Adventurers. I didn't really find any of that plot funny or that interesting, and the parallels between their secret society and the secret society of revolutionaries is an idea that's there, but feels completely half-baked and underdone.

3. I thought it was a huge missed opportunity to have Willa's genetic parentage be such a nothingburger to either her or DiCaprio. Did she ever tell him Lockjaw was her dad? If not, why not? We never see her actually reckon with the emotional weight of nearly being killed by her biological dad, having a white supremacist biological dad in the first place, or how that affects her relationship with Bob going forward. And we never get to see Bob accept her regardless of who her biological father is. It's a huge missing part of the resolution of the film, and the letter from Perfidia has zero emotional weight to me because she's been so absent from the story and the letter literally does nothing to resolve any emotional or story arcs in the film. You could excise the whole scene of her getting the letter and it wouldn't change the actual story or characters one bit.

4. Speaking of unnecessary scenes, Lockjaw's final fate is a waste of time. We've literally already seen his assassination attempt by the Christmas Adventurers and how they reject any idea of him joining... why would they invite him to their secret offices to kill him there? Isn't that more fishy than a random hit on a desert highway? Why expose themselves like that? And even disregarding the logic of the scene, what story purpose does it have? It's telling us something that we already know... that their little club has decided to kill Lockjaw. It's not funny, surprising or cathartic (to me at least), so why not have him just die in his car like we already assumed?

5. There are far more interesting characters in the periphery of the story. The nunnery, Del Toro, Regina Hall, the whole siege sequence on the high school and town, even Willa herself... all of these felt like more interesting story threads than Bob's. PTA does love to build out ensembles with compelling side characters, but here I felt like Bob and Lockjaw weren't nearly interesting enough to carry the bulk of the story.



Which is all a shame because I think there's plenty to like about the film, and ultimately I liked more than I didn't. But I never emotionally connected with the characters, or thought the ideas and themes were built out enough to make something gripping. I feel like a lot of the positive responses I've read about it (especially on here) are people broadcasting their own ideas and themes onto what at times feels like a sketch of a story that doesn't do nearly enough work itself.
  - Point '4' I very much agree with this. Although I enjoyed the film's message about 'true' activism being compassion and community in place of 'showy' loud 'revolutionaries' and how PTA skewers both 'sides' as betraying themselves, there were too many loose ends and dead end threads.

Especially the final act missed chances for catharsis, or rounding out characters, with the Lockjaw one especially egregious - I thought the obvious move was that he was going to go and kill the other racists, but someone it was even less original than that?

There wasn't even breathing room between him being alive and then getting done in - by the same exact people, as you said - so it seemed all for the point of a joke on how he was reverse r@ped? He'd already been betrayed so that note being hit again - instantly after - was really lazy to me. And why would he not suspect - or follow up on the man who shot him in the fucking face? He was for some reason still loyal? Is that some message about "patriots"?

Meanwhile, people like Sensei are just forgotten about after being arrested, as are all the other elements like the sisters. Good but it's not a masterpiece for, the deeper elements weren't nuanced or explored enough and the bigger pieces weren't big enough to be engaging or exciting to say it's a 'big' thriller movie.
  - The Willa arc is so underbaked. Conceptually I can argue that her parentage was never again addressed to stress how much it doesn’t matter — Bob is her dad, period, and Willa doesn’t have to reckon with her Lockjaw experience because Bob being her dad is enough to heal her from it — but that’s hardly satisfying as drama or psychology. In a film that cared about Willa as a character rather than a prop for Bob’s fulfillment, she’d be changed by such a primal revelation and have to grapple with it further. Does Bob know, what if he did, would he still love me…? Even her trying for a moment to tell him until he suggests that he already knew would help, dramatically.

In any case, wistfully reading a letter from her absent rat mother who (she just learned) cheated on her dad with a white supremacist doesn’t make much sense. Having recently re-embraced her dad, she should be extra mad at her mom for betraying him.
  - Yeah, it’s one of PTA’s weaker films and very very on the nose
  - The whole film is a massive missed opportunity. It's a real shame considering the potential that was at hand. I enjoyed reading your review. You broke it down succinctly enough, while still hammering away at the flaws that plague this film.
  - I agree with this so strongly
4. weak third act. that’s what was my complaint. all that build up only to have a very lackluster final ending. it’s sad we didn’t see chase do karate and we didn’t see DiCaprio do explosions.
  - It’s purposeful that DiCaprio didn’t use explosions. PTA said the audience expects him to channel all the revolutionary stuff he did back in the day but the point is that he’s been lazy for so long that he can’t. He lost it. All he can really do is try and be there for his daughter.
